en.extract:
The blue-bearded bee-eater (Nyctyornis athertoni) is a species of bee-eater found in much of the Indian subcontinent and parts of Southeast Asia. This bee-eater is found in forest clearings. It is found mainly in the Malayan region but extends west into peninsular India. The blue feathers of its throat are elongated and often fluffed giving it its name. They have a loud call but are not as gregarious or active as the smaller bee-eaters, and their square ended tail lacks the typical "wires" made up of the shafts of the longer central tail feathers found in many other bee-eaters.


Description

This is probably the largest species of the bee-eater family. Adults measure 31 to 35 cm (12 to 14 in) in length and weigh 70 to 93 g (2.5 to 3.3 oz). This species has a large sickle shaped bill and the square ended tail lacks the "wires" that are typical of smaller bee-eaters. The bird is grass green with a turquoise forehead, face and chin. The feathers of the throat are elongated giving it a bearded appearance when they are fluffed out. The belly is yellowish to olive with streaks of green or blue. The peninsular Indian populations are said to be paler green than the northeast Indian populations. Although males and females appear similar, the blue throat feathers of the male show higher ultraviolet reflectivity than those of the female.
The species is named after Lieut. John Atherton (13th Light Dragoons, died in 1827) a nephew of Mrs. P. J. Selby who obtained a specimen of the bird. Selby described the species in "Illustrations of Ornithology" published along with Sir William Jardine in 1828. Jardine and Selby described it in the Illustrations of Ornithology (Series 1, Volume 2 part 4, November 1828, plate 58) and the type locality (holotype is in the Selby Collection, UMZC, 25/Mer/7/b/2) was said to be Cachar District Assam by E. C. Stuart Baker but Sir N B Kinnear re-designated Bangalore as the type locality for the species based on the fact that Atherton was posted in Bangalore when he wrote to Selby and noted that he was helped by a French collector (thought to be Leschenault). However the species is rare in that region. Atherton informed Selby that the bird was very rare, found in the thickest jungles, feeding at night and noisy with "curr, curr" calls.
The nominate form is found in India and parts of mainland Southeast Asia while brevicaudatus is an insular population from Hainan. A subspecies bartletti from northeastern India, described by W. N. Koelz, is usually considered part of the nominate population.


Distribution and habitat
This species is found in a variety of habitats mostly at medium altitudes but below 2000m altitude. Thin to fairly thick forest in medium elevations with clearings is the typical habitat. It is found singly or in small groups of up to three and is very patchily distributed. Their presence in an area can easily be missed. It has been reported from the hill regions of the Satpuras, Western Ghats, Eastern Ghats, Nilgiris, Chota Nagpur and from the Sub-Himalayan forests.


Behaviour and ecology

This bird has a loud call, but does not call frequently. It is also not as active as the smaller bee-eaters. The calls include cackling hornbill like calls, a dry "Kit-tik... Kit-tik" in a series or hollow nasal "kyao" calls. Pairs may engage in duets of cackling and rattling which ends in short purring notes. The flight is undulating and very barbet-like.
The breeding season is February to August in India and courtship involves ritual feeding, bowing and tail fanning. Nest excavation may begin a month before the laying of eggs. The nest is a deep tunnel in a mud bank within which four very spherical and white eggs are laid.
The species appears to feed mainly on bees. It exploits the defensive behavior of Giant honey bee (Apis dorsata) colonies by provoking the mass release of guard bees which are then caught in the air and eaten as they pursue the bird. Although mainly foraging using aerial sallies, it is known to sometimes glean from bark. They may sometimes associate with mixed-species foraging flocks. Birds have been seen at flowers of Erythrina and Salmalia although it is unclear whether they fed on nectar or insects attracted to the flowers.
A blood parasite Leucocytozoon nyctyornis has been described from this species and feather parasites in the genus Brueelia are also known.


References

Vol 17}} | range_map = Nyctyornis athertoni extant map.png | range_map_caption = Distribution of the ''Nyctyornis athertoni'' }} The '''blue-bearded bee-eater''' ('''''Nyctyornis athertoni''''') is a species of [[bee-eater]] found in much of the [[Indian subcontinent]] and parts of [[Southeast Asia]]. This bee-eater is found in forest clearings. It is found mainly in the [[Malay Peninsula|Malayan region]] but extends west into peninsular [[India]]. The blue feathers of its throat are elongated and often fluffed giving it its name. They have a loud call but are not as gregarious or active as the smaller bee-eaters, and their square ended tail lacks the typical "wires" made up of the shafts of the longer central tail feathers found in many other bee-eaters. ==Description== [[File:Blue-bearded bee-eater, Bangladesh.jpg|thumb|Nominate subspecies perched on an ''Erythrina'' sp., [[Bangladesh]] |upright|left]] This is probably the largest species of the bee-eater family. Adults measure {{convert|31|to|35|cm|in|abbr=on}} in length and weigh {{convert|70|to|93|g|oz|abbr=on}}.Fry, H. and G. M. Kirwan (2020). ''Blue-bearded Bee-eater (Nyctyornis athertoni)'', version 1.0. In Birds of the World (J. del Hoyo, A. Elliott, J. Sargatal, D. A. Christie, and E. de Juana, Editors). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA.{{cite book |title=CRC Handbook of Avian Body Masses |edition=2nd |editor-first=John B. Jr. |editor-last=Dunning |publisher=CRC Press |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-4200-6444-5}} This species has a large sickle shaped bill and the square ended tail lacks the "wires" that are typical of smaller bee-eaters. The bird is grass green with a turquoise forehead, face and chin. The feathers of the throat are elongated giving it a bearded appearance when they are fluffed out. The belly is yellowish to olive with streaks of green or blue. The [[peninsular India]]n populations are said to be paler green than the [[northeast India]]n populations.{{cite book|author1=Rasmussen PC |author2=JC Anderton |name-list-style=amp|year=2005|title=Birds of South Asia: The Ripley Guide.|publisher=Smithsonian Institution & Lynx Edicions|volume=2|page=268}} Although males and females appear similar, the blue throat feathers of the male show higher ultraviolet reflectivity than those of the female.{{cite journal|title=The ubiquity of avian ultraviolet plumage reflectance|author1=Eaton, Muir D. |author2=Scott M. Lanyon |name-list-style=amp|journal=Proc. R. Soc. Lond. B |year=2003|volume=270|pages=1721–1726|doi=10.1098/rspb.2003.2431|pmid=12965000|issue=1525|pmc=1691429}} The species is named after Lieut. John Atherton ([[13th Hussars|13th Light Dragoons]], died in 1827) a nephew of Mrs. [[Prideaux John Selby|P. J. Selby]] who obtained a specimen of the bird. Selby described the species in ''"Illustrations of Ornithology"'' published along with Sir [[Sir William Jardine, 7th Baronet|William Jardine]] in 1828.{{cite book|first=Christine Elisabeth|last=Jackson|author2=Peter Davis|year=2001|title=Sir William Jardine: a life in natural history|publisher=Continuum International|isbn=0-7185-0164-0|page=208}} Jardine and Selby described it in the Illustrations of Ornithology (Series 1, Volume 2 part 4, November 1828, plate 58) and the type locality (holotype is in the Selby Collection, UMZC, 25/Mer/7/b/2) was said to be Cachar District [[Assam]] by E. C. Stuart Baker{{cite journal|author=Baker, ECS |year=1922| title=Hand-list of the "Birds of India". Part 5. |journal= J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.|volume= 28|pages=313–333|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/30112876}} but [[Norman Boyd Kinnear|Sir N B Kinnear]] re-designated Bangalore as the type locality for the species based on the fact that Atherton was posted in Bangalore when he wrote to Selby and noted that he was helped by a French collector (thought to be [[Jean-Baptiste Leschenault de La Tour|Leschenault]]).{{cite journal|author=Kinnear, NB|year=1925|journal=Ibis|pages=751–753|title=Letters and notes: Type-localities|doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.1925.tb02952.x}} However the species is rare in that region.{{cite journal|author1=Karthikeyan, S |author2=Prasad, JN |year=1993|title= Recent sighting of Bluebearded Bee-eater ''Nyctyornis athertoni'' (Jardine & Selby) |journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc. |volume=90|issue=2|pages=290–291|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48609545}} Atherton informed Selby that the bird was very rare, found in the thickest jungles, feeding at night and noisy with "''curr, curr''" calls.{{cite book|author=Dresser, H.E.|url=https://archive.org/details/monographmeropi00dres/page/9/mode/1up/|title=A monograph of the Meropidae, or the family of bee-eaters|year=1886|place=London|pages=9–10}} The nominate form is found in [[India]] and parts of mainland [[Southeast Asia]] while ''brevicaudatus'' is an insular population from Hainan. A subspecies ''bartletti'' from northeastern India, described by [[Walter Norman Koelz|W. N. Koelz]], is usually considered part of the nominate population.{{cite journal| author=Storer, R.W.|year=1988|journal=Miscellaneous Publication, University of Michigan Museum of Zoology|volume=174|pages=1–69|title=Type specimens of birds in the collections of the University of Michigan Museum of Zoology|hdl=2027.42/56418}}{{cite journal|author=Marien, Daniel |year=1950|title= Notes on some Asiatic Meropidae (birds)| journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.|volume=49|issue=2|pages=151–164|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48181577}} ==Distribution and habitat== [[File:Blue bearded Bee eater.jpg|thumb|left|upright|The feathers of the chin are long and sometimes raised up into a "beard".]]This species is found in a variety of habitats mostly at medium altitudes but below 2000m altitude. Thin to fairly thick forest in medium elevations with clearings is the typical habitat. It is found singly or in small groups of up to three and is very patchily distributed. Their presence in an area can easily be missed.{{cite journal|author=Inglis, Charles M|year=1949|title=The Bluebearded Bee-eater (''Alcemerops athertoni'' Jard. & Selby) on the Nilgiris|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48732147|journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.|volume=48|issue=3|pages=581–582}} It has been reported from the hill regions of the Satpuras, Western Ghats, Eastern Ghats, Nilgiris, Chota Nagpur and from the Sub-Himalayan forests.{{cite journal|author= Osmaston, BB |year=1922 |title= The occurrence of the Bluebearded Bee-eater ''Nyctiornis athertoni'' in the Central Provinces |journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.|volume= 28|issue=3|page=805|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/52170415}}{{cite journal|author=Hewetson, C|year=1944|title=Bearded Bee-eater (''Alcemerops athertoni'') in the Central Provinces|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48295263|journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.|volume=44|issue=4|pages=592–593}}{{cite journal|author=Ara, Jamal |author-link=Jamal Ara|year=1951 | title= Distribution of the Blue-bearded Bee-eater [''Nyctiornis athertoni'' (Jardine & Selby)] |journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.|volume=50|issue=1|pages=175–176|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48056678}} ==Behaviour and ecology== [[File:Bluebearded Bee-eater.jpg|thumb|The nest tunnel excavated in a vertical mud bank|alt=|left]] This bird has a loud call, but does not call frequently. It is also not as active as the smaller bee-eaters. The calls include cackling hornbill like calls, a dry "Kit-tik... Kit-tik" in a series or hollow nasal "kyao" calls. Pairs may engage in duets of cackling and rattling which ends in short purring notes. The flight is undulating and very barbet-like.{{cite book|author1=Ali S |author2=SD Ripley |name-list-style=amp|year=1983|title=Handbook of the birds of India and Pakistan. Volume 4|pages=112–113|publisher=Oxford University Press}} The breeding season is February to August in India and courtship involves ritual feeding, bowing and tail fanning. Nest excavation may begin a month before the laying of eggs. The nest is a deep tunnel in a mud bank within which four very spherical and white eggs are laid.{{cite book|editor=Blanford, WT|title=Fauna of British India. Birds Vol. 3|year=1895|pages=115–116|url=https://archive.org/stream/faunaofbritishin03oate#page/116/mode/1up}} The species appears to feed mainly on bees.{{cite journal|author=Deignan HG |title=The birds of northern Thailand|year=1945|pages=208–209|url=https://archive.org/stream/bulletinunitedst1861945unit#page/209/mode/1up|journal=Smithsonian Institution Bulletin|volume=186}} It exploits the defensive behavior of [[Giant honey bee]] (''[[Apis dorsata]]'') colonies by provoking the mass release of guard bees which are then caught in the air and eaten as they pursue the bird.{{cite journal |author1=Gerald Kastberger |author2=D.K. Sharma | title=The predator-prey interaction between blue-bearded bee eaters (Nyctyornis athertoni Jardine and Selby 1830) and giant honeybees (Apis dorsata Fabricius 1798) | journal=Apidologie | volume=31 | pages=727–736 | publisher=EDP Sciences | date=2000-09-22 | url=http://www.apidologie.org/articles/apido/pdf/2000/06/m0608.pdf | access-date=2008-07-27 | doi=10.1051/apido:2000157 | issue=6 | doi-access=free }} Although mainly foraging using [[Hawking (birds)|aerial sallies]], it is known to sometimes [[Gleaning (birds)|glean]] from bark.{{cite journal|author=Santharam, V |year=1999|title=Birds foraging on tree trunks|journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc. |volume=96|issue=3|pages=468–469|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48583155}} They may sometimes associate with [[mixed-species foraging flock]]s.{{cite journal|author1=Fleming, RL |author2=M A Traylor |name-list-style=amp|title=Distributional notes on Nepal birds|journal=Fieldiana|volume=53|issue=3| year=1968| pages=147–203 |url=https://archive.org/stream/distributionalno533flem#page/194/mode/2up}} Birds have been seen at flowers of ''[[Erythrina]]'' and ''[[Salmalia]]'' although it is unclear whether they fed on nectar or insects attracted to the flowers. A blood parasite ''Leucocytozoon nyctyornis'' has been described from this species{{cite journal|title=''Leucocytozoon nyctyornis'' n. sp. from bluebearded bee-eater ''Nyctyornis athertoni'' (Jardine and Selby)|author=Nandi NC|journal=Archiv für Protistenkunde|year=1986|volume=132|issue=2|pages=113–117|doi=10.1016/s0003-9365(86)80013-6}} and feather parasites in the genus ''[[Brueelia]]'' are also known.{{cite journal|title=The Brueelia (Mallophaga: Philopteridae) of the Meropidae (Aves: Coraciiformes)|author=Williams NS|journal=Journal of the Kansas Entomological Society|volume=54|issue=3 |year=1981 |pages=510–518}} ==References== {{Reflist|2}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Nyctyornis athertoni}} {{Wikispecies|Nyctyornis athertoni}} *[http://ibc.lynxeds.com/species/blue-bearded-bee-eater-nyctyornis-athertoni Internet Bird Collection] * [http://www.xeno-canto.org/asia/species.php?query=Nyctyornis+athertoni Calls] * {{usurped|1=[https://web.archive.org/web/20201024125420/http://orientalbirdimages.org/search.php?Bird_ID=358&Bird_Image_ID=172612&Location= Specimen photos]}} {{Bee-eaters}} {{Taxonbar|from=Q882415}} [[Category:Meropidae|blue-bearded bee-eater]] [[Category:Birds of Bhutan]] [[Category:Birds of India]] [[Category:Birds of Nepal]] [[Category:Birds of Hainan]] [[Category:Birds of Yunnan]] [[Category:Birds of Southeast Asia]] [[Category:Birds described in 1830|blue-bearded bee-eater]] [[Category:Taxa named by Sir William Jardine]] [[Category:Taxa named by Prideaux John Selby|blue-bearded bee-eater]] [[Category:Articles containing video clips]]

zh.extract:
蓝须夜蜂虎（学名：Nyctyornis athertoni）为蜂虎科夜蜂虎属的鸟类。该物种的模式产地在孟加拉国。


亚种
蓝须夜蜂虎指名亚种（学名：Nyctyornis athertoni athertoni）。在中国大陆，分布于云南等地。该物种的模式产地在孟加拉国。
蓝须夜蜂虎海南亚种（学名：Nyctyornis athertoni brevicaudata）。在中国大陆，分布于海南等地。该物种的模式产地在海南。
